front: Figure: this king's name is traditionally glossed as meaning "wise elf"
Figure: first West Saxon king to style himself "King of the Anglo-Saxons," after uniting Wessex with English Mercia
Battle: decisive 878 victory over Guthrum's Viking army, Alfred the Great's defining military triumph
Event: before this battle, Alfred hid out at a fortress on the Isle of Athelney, later toured by his biographer Bishop Asser
Figure: Alfred's opponent at the Battle of Edington, Guthrum the Old, was linked to this Viking commander
Treaty: established the Danelaw, with a boundary running along Watling Street
Treaty: required Guthrum's baptism under the Christian name Aethelstan and his adoption as Alfred's godson
Treaty: set compensation for unlawful killing at eight half-marks of gold
Treaty: named a boundary fortress called "Cairceri" in the kingdom of the Hwicce
Treaty: divided the kingdom of Mercia between Alfred and Guthrum
Treaty: its signing is conflated with the separate Peace of Wedmore and agreement at Chippenham in the fifty-sixth chapter of Asser's biography
Battle: before becoming king, Alfred allegedly rallied troops here with the "Blowing Stone"; the Viking leader Bagsecg and his five earls were killed
Law: this law code, compiled by Alfred, built upon the earlier codes of Aethelberht of Kent and Offa of Mercia
Institution: network of fortified towns Alfred built to defend Wessex from Viking attack, recorded in the Burghal Hidage
Event: Alfred's burh defense network was extended after his death by these two of his children
Work: Old English translation of Boethius's Consolation of Philosophy, whose preface describes translating "sometimes word for word, sometimes sense for sense"
back: The Old English Boethius (Alfred the Great)

front: Work: translation of Pope Gregory the Great's Pastoral Care, prefaced with a call to translate texts "most necessary for all men to know"
Work: chronicle first compiled during Alfred's reign, providing an ongoing year-by-year record of Anglo-Saxon history
Work: Marie de France claimed her Anglo-Norman Aesop's Fables were translated from Alfred's Old English version of this collection
Work: translation of this historian's work that incorporated the Norwegian traveler Ohthere's firsthand account of walrus hunting
Work: collection of apocryphal wise sayings attributed to Alfred, quoted in the Middle English poem The Owl and the Nightingale
Work: Alfred the Great's main medieval biography, the "Life of King Alfred," was written by this Welsh monk
Event: later legend holds that Alfred burned a peasant woman's cakes while in hiding from the Danes, a story absent from Asser's original biography
Event: legendarily found an infant he named Nestingum in an eagle's nest, wearing gold armbands and wrapped in a purple cloak
Artifact: gold-encased enamel and rock-crystal jewel discovered near Athelney, now held in the Ashmolean Museum
Document: medical text recording remedies used by one Elias of Jerusalem to treat Alfred, possibly for Crohn's disease
Document: this manuscript takes its name from a letter of recommendation Alfred wrote for the monk it honors
Institution: convent founded by Alfred's wife Ealhswith in Winchester, the city protected by his largest fortification
